title: Lower Passage Lamps
desc: The hidden route under the inner ring stays useful only if its guide lamps and coded marks stay ahead of damp and rumor.

steps.text:
Kiru City's secret passages are less dramatic than outsiders imagine and more important than they realize. Most are narrow service runs under the inner ring, lined with shielded niches, coded chalk marks, and doors that look like storage walls until someone touches the right latch. The lower passage you have been sent to smells of old stone and lamp oil. It is quiet in the practical way of places that are meant to remain in use without attracting stories.

The passage warden lifts a hooded lantern and checks the soot line inside the glass. "Replace the shielded lamps if you want the cleaner task," she says. "Or refresh the route marks and latch codes if you would rather deal with the walls. We do not need panic down here. We need people finding the correct door in the dark."

options.label: Replace the shielded lamps along the lower passage
Refresh the route arrows and latch marks on the walls

steps.text:
The passage lamps are built to hide their light from the wrong angles and preserve it at the right ones, which means they fail quietly when neglected. You trim blackened wicks, refill the shallow oil cups, and swap two cracked glasses whose haze had turned guidance into suggestion. As each hood closes, the corridor gains another patient pool of amber light, enough to mark the floor and the next turning without letting brightness leak toward the surface seams above.

When the last niche is lit, the run no longer feels abandoned. It feels maintained, which is the quality secret infrastructure depends on most. The warden walks the line once, watching how the lamps overlap, then rests the lantern hook at her belt. "Good," she says. "A hidden route should be easy for the right people and uninteresting to everyone else."

options.label: Latch the oil chest and leave the lamps burning evenly

steps.text:
The route marks are simple on purpose: arrows at ankle height, coded circles at the latches, and a few narrow chalk bars to distinguish storage alcoves from doors that actually matter. Damp and passing boots have blurred half of them into uncertainty. You clean the stone, redraw the arrows in the proper sequence, and renew the latch codes with just enough contrast for a practiced eye to catch them quickly. The passage remains secret. It simply stops being ambiguous.

The warden tests one of the wall doors by lantern light, follows the fresh marks to the next turn, and allows herself a thin expression that may be satisfaction or merely relief. In a city built on visible order, the hidden systems need their own version of the same thing. "Better," she says. "Now the foundations can keep their promises."

options.label: Cap the chalk and leave the passage legible again
